        self.latencies.append(latency_ms)
        self.n_requests += 1
        if error: self.n_errors += 1

    def psi(self, buckets=10):
        if self.reference is None or len(self.scores) < 100: return 0.0
        bp = np.percentile(self.reference, np.linspace(0,100,buckets+1))
        bp[0]=-np.inf; bp[-1]=np.inf
        e = np.histogram(self.reference, bp)[0]/len(self.reference)+1e-9
        a = np.histogram(list(self.scores), bp)[0]/len(self.scores)+1e-9
        return float(np.sum((a-e)*np.log(a/e)))

    def report(self):
        print(f"Requests: {self.n_requests}  Errors: {self.n_errors/max(1,self.n_requests)*100:.1f}%")
        print(f"Score p50={np.percentile(self.scores,50):.3f}  "
              f"p95={np.percentile(self.scores,95):.3f}")
        print(f"Latency p99={np.percentile(self.latencies,99):.1f}ms")
        p = self.psi()
        drift_s = "DRIFT!" if p > self.psi_thresh else "OK"
        print(f"PSI={p:.3f}  {drift_s}")

monitor = ModelMonitor()
np.random.seed(0)
monitor.set_reference(np.random.beta(2,5,10000))
for _ in range(500): monitor.record(np.random.beta(3,3), np.random.exponential(5))
monitor.report()
concepts.plain: ML monitoring is about knowing whether your deployed model is still doing its job. Unlike traditional software monitoring (is it running?), ML monitoring asks: are predictions still accurate? Is the input data changing? Is the model's confidence calibrated? You need different tools to answer these.

concepts.title: Retraining Pipelines
concepts.short: Automated workflows that periodically or event-driven retrain models on fresh data to maintain production accuracy.
concepts.body:
A retraining pipeline automates the end-to-end process of refreshing a deployed model: data extraction → feature computation → training → evaluation → validation → registry update → deployment promotion. Without automation, retraining is manual, infrequent, and error-prone. Automated pipelines allow teams to maintain model freshness at scale — retraining daily or even continuously — without per-cycle engineering effort.

Triggers for retraining are either time-based (retrain every N days regardless of drift) or metric-based (retrain when monitored metrics breach thresholds). Time-based triggers are simple but wasteful if drift is slow and expensive if data is scarce. Metric-based triggers are more efficient but require robust monitoring. A validation gate in the pipeline prevents regressions: the new model is compared against the current production model on a recent holdout set, and promotion only proceeds if the new model is not worse by more than a defined threshold.

Pipeline orchestration tools (Airflow, Prefect, Metaflow, Kubeflow Pipelines) manage dependency ordering, retries, and environment isolation. Each stage runs in a reproducible container with pinned dependencies. Training data versioning (DVC, Delta Lake, Iceberg) ensures that models can be re-trained from the exact same data snapshot for debugging. Feature stores provide the point-in-time-correct data joins. The registry stores the output. Continuous training is the extreme case: models update continuously on a stream of new data using online learning algorithms.
concepts.code_example:
# Simplified retraining pipeline using Python (Prefect/Airflow-style)
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any
import numpy as np
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score

@dataclass
class PipelineResult:
    success: bool
    auc: float
    model: Any
    reason: str = ""

def extract_data(days_back=30):
    np.random.seed(42)
    X = np.random.randn(2000, 10)
    y = (X[:,0] - X[:,1] + np.random.randn(2000)*0.5 > 0).astype(int)
    return X, y

def train_model(X, y):
    split = int(len(X)*0.8)
    model = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, learning_rate=0.1)
    model.fit(X[:split], y[:split])
    return model, X[split:], y[split:]

def validate(model, X_val, y_val, prod_auc, min_improvement=-0.01):
    auc = roc_auc_score(y_val, model.predict_proba(X_val)[:,1])
    ok  = (auc - prod_auc) >= min_improvement
    return auc, ok

def run_pipeline(prod_auc=0.75):
    X, y          = extract_data()
    model, Xv, yv = train_model(X, y)
    auc, ok       = validate(model, Xv, yv, prod_auc)
    if ok: print(f"PROMOTE: new AUC={auc:.4f} vs prod={prod_auc:.4f}")
    else:  print(f"REJECT:  new AUC={auc:.4f} < prod={prod_auc:.4f}")
    return PipelineResult(success=ok, auc=auc, model=model if ok else None)

result = run_pipeline()
concepts.plain: A retraining pipeline automatically refreshes a model when its performance degrades. Instead of a one-time training job that someone runs manually, it triggers on a schedule or when drift is detected, trains on fresh data, validates the new model, and promotes it — all without manual intervention.

concepts.title: Vector Databases
concepts.short: Databases purpose-built for storing and querying high-dimensional vector embeddings at scale.
concepts.body:
Vector databases store embedding vectors alongside metadata and expose APIs for similarity search (ANN), filtering (metadata constraints + vector similarity), and CRUD operations. They differ from general-purpose databases by using specialized ANN indexes (HNSW, IVF) as first-class citizens. Metadata filtering — 'find the 10 most similar vectors where category=electronics and price < 100' — requires hybrid indexes combining vector similarity with scalar attribute filters. Doing this correctly without scanning the full dataset is the core engineering challenge.

Major options: Pinecone (managed, production-ready, scalable to billions), Weaviate (open-source, supports multiple index types, GraphQL API), Chroma (lightweight, embedded, excellent for prototyping), Qdrant (Rust-based, open-source, high-performance on-premise), Milvus (open-source, cloud-native, horizontally scalable). All provide the same core primitives: collection/index creation, upsert vectors with IDs and metadata, query by vector, filter by metadata. PostgreSQL with the pgvector extension supports vector operations in a familiar RDBMS, eliminating an additional infrastructure component for smaller workloads.

Design decisions: index type (HNSW for high recall/speed, IVF for lower memory), quantization (PQ/SQ for memory reduction), dimensionality (higher dims = better representation but more memory/latency), replication and sharding strategy for fault tolerance and horizontal scaling. In RAG pipelines, the vector database holds document chunks; at query time, the user query is embedded and the ANN search retrieves the most relevant chunks for the LLM context.
concepts.code_example:
# pip install chromadb
import chromadb
from chromadb.utils import embedding_functions
import numpy as np

# Embedded Chroma (no server needed)
client = chromadb.Client()

# Create a collection
col = client.create_collection("ml_concepts")

# Add documents with metadata
docs  = [
    "Gradient descent minimizes the loss by following the negative gradient",
    "Attention mechanisms allow models to focus on relevant parts of input",
    "Regularization prevents overfitting by penalizing model complexity",
    "Backpropagation computes gradients via the chain rule",
    "Transformers use self-attention instead of recurrence",
]
metas = [{"topic": t} for t in ["optimization","nlp","training","optimization","nlp"]]
# Use random embeddings (normally: model.encode(docs))
np.random.seed(42)
embeds = [np.random.randn(64).tolist() for _ in docs]

col.add(documents=docs, metadatas=metas,
        ids=[f"doc{i}" for i in range(len(docs))],
        embeddings=embeds)

# Query by vector
query_embed = np.random.randn(64).tolist()
results = col.query(query_embeddings=[query_embed], n_results=2)
print("Top matches:")
for doc, dist in zip(results["documents"][0], results["distances"][0]):
    print(f"  [{dist:.3f}] {doc[:50]}...")
concepts.plain: A vector database stores numerical representations of text or images and lets you search them by meaning, not exact keywords. 'Find me documents that mean the same as this query' — even if they use completely different words. It's the engine behind semantic search and a core building block of RAG systems.

concepts.title: Tokenization
concepts.short: Splitting text into discrete token units that a language model processes as input.
concepts.body:
Tokenization converts raw text into sequences of integer IDs a model can process. Character-level tokenization is simple but produces very long sequences; word-level tokenization struggles with rare words and morphology. Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) is the dominant approach: start with individual bytes, iteratively merge the most frequent adjacent pair into a new token, repeating until the vocabulary size is reached. This produces a vocabulary of common words and subword pieces, naturally handling rare words by decomposing them.

The vocabulary size (typically 32k–128k tokens) and the tokenization of a string interact to determine model cost: every token costs one position of context window and one forward-pass step during generation. English text averages ~4 characters per token with GPT-4's tokenizer. Code tokenizes differently than prose — identifiers, whitespace, and punctuation all affect token count. Tokenization is language-specific: languages with richer morphology (Finnish, Turkish) or non-Latin scripts (Chinese, Arabic) require careful vocabulary design to avoid unfair per-language token efficiency.

Tokenizers must be deterministic and invertible: encoding then decoding should reproduce the original text. Special tokens ([CLS], [SEP], <|endoftext|>, <|im_start|>) signal structural boundaries and roles to the model. The tokenizer is a fixed preprocessing artifact shipped with the model — changing it would make the learned embedding table incompatible. tiktoken (OpenAI), HuggingFace tokenizers (Rust-based, fast), and SentencePiece are the main libraries.
concepts.code_example:
import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")  # GPT-4 tokenizer

texts = [
    "The attention mechanism is fundamental to transformers.",
    "Supercalifragilisticexpialidocious",
    "def train_loop(model, optimizer, dataloader):",
    "\u4eba\u5de5\u667a\u80fd",  # Chinese: artificial intelligence
]
for text in texts:
    tokens = enc.encode(text)
    decoded = [enc.decode([t]) for t in tokens]
    print(f"Text:    {text[:50]}")
    print(f"Tokens:  {tokens}")
    print(f"Pieces:  {decoded}")
    print(f"Count:   {len(tokens)}\n")

# Roundtrip
orig = "Hello, world! This is a test."
assert enc.decode(enc.encode(orig)) == orig
print("Roundtrip OK")
concepts.plain: Tokenisation is how a language model reads text — it breaks words into sub-word pieces called tokens. 'unbelievable' might become ['un', 'believ', 'able']. The model never sees actual letters or words — it sees token IDs. Understanding tokenisation explains why models struggle with unusual spellings and character-level tasks.

concepts.title: Embeddings
concepts.short: Dense vector representations of tokens or text that encode semantic meaning in a continuous space.
concepts.body:
An embedding layer maps each token ID to a learned dense vector in ℝᵈ. At initialisation the embedding table is random; during training, vectors for tokens that appear in similar contexts are pulled together. Word2Vec (2013) demonstrated this explicitly: training a shallow network to predict surrounding words from a center word (skip-gram) or vice versa (CBOW) produces vectors encoding semantic analogies. GloVe learns from global co-occurrence statistics. Both produce static embeddings — the same word always maps to the same vector regardless of context.

Contextual embeddings from transformer models (BERT, GPT) produce different vectors for the same token depending on surrounding context. 'bank' in 'river bank' and 'bank account' get different representations. These richer representations made contextual embeddings dramatically more useful for downstream tasks. For retrieval and semantic similarity, sentence embeddings — single vectors representing entire sentences — are computed by mean-pooling token embeddings or using a dedicated [CLS] token, then fine-tuned with contrastive losses (SimCSE, E5, BGE).

Practically, embeddings are the bridge between the discrete symbolic world of text and the continuous world of neural computation. The embedding dimension (typically 512–4096 for LLMs) is a core architectural hyperparameter — larger models use larger embedding dimensions. At inference, the embedding table is a large matrix multiply that maps a one-hot token index to its dense vector. Modern LLMs tie the input embedding weights to the output projection layer (weight tying), halving the parameter count for a major component with minimal quality loss.
concepts.plain: Embeddings turn words, sentences, or images into lists of numbers that capture meaning. Words with similar meanings get similar numbers; unrelated words get very different numbers. This is what lets machines compare meaning mathematically — it's how search, recommendations, and semantic similarity all work under the hood.

concepts.title: Transformer Architecture
concepts.short: The full encoder-decoder architecture built on multi-head attention that underlies modern language models.
concepts.body:
The original transformer has an encoder stack and a decoder stack, each with N identical layers. Each encoder layer applies multi-head self-attention then a position-wise FFN, with residual connections and layer normalization wrapping each sub-layer (pre-LN or post-LN, with pre-LN now preferred for training stability). The decoder additionally has a causal self-attention layer and a cross-attention layer that attends to encoder outputs. Residual connections (x + sublayer(x)) enable gradient flow across many layers; layer normalization stabilises activations.

Modern LLMs are almost universally decoder-only: no encoder, no cross-attention, just a stack of causal self-attention + FFN blocks. This simplifies the architecture and the training objective (next-token prediction). The FFN sublayer uses a dimension expansion of 4x (or 8x/3x for SwiGLU variants): FFN(x) = W₂·GELU(W₁x). For a 7B parameter model with d=4096, the FFN alone accounts for ~50% of parameters. GQA (Grouped Query Attention) shares key-value heads across query heads to reduce KV-cache memory during inference without significant quality loss.

Key architectural choices that separate modern LLMs from the 2017 transformer: RoPE positional encoding (relative, length-generalising) instead of learned absolute positions; RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm (no mean subtraction, faster); SwiGLU activation instead of ReLU/GeLU (three linear layers, empirically better); GQA or MQA for memory-efficient attention; Flash Attention for hardware-efficient exact attention computation. These changes are largely implementation details but collectively contribute 10-20% throughput and quality improvements.
concepts.plain: The Transformer is the architecture behind almost every modern AI model. Its key innovation is attention: instead of processing tokens one by one, it directly compares every token to every other token in the sequence simultaneously. This parallelism makes it fast to train and effective at capturing long-range relationships.

concepts.title: Pretraining
concepts.short: Large-scale self-supervised training on massive text corpora to give a model broad language and world knowledge.
concepts.body:
Pretraining is the phase where a model learns from raw text without human labels. For decoder-only models, the objective is next-token prediction (causal language modelling): given tokens t₁…tₙ₋₁, maximise the log-probability of tₙ. This deceptively simple objective forces the model to internalise grammar, factual knowledge, reasoning patterns, and code structure across trillions of tokens. The training corpus is carefully curated from web crawls (CommonCrawl, C4), books, code (GitHub), Wikipedia, and other sources, with extensive deduplication and quality filtering.

Scaling laws (Chinchilla, 2022) describe how loss scales with model size N and training tokens D: for a given compute budget C ≈ 6ND, optimal performance is achieved by training a model ~3x smaller than previously thought on ~3x more tokens. Chinchilla-optimal models match larger undertrained models at the same compute cost. In practice, frontier labs train beyond Chinchilla-optimal because inference cost (serving smaller models) matters as much as training cost.

Precision and hardware: pretraining uses mixed precision (bf16 activations, fp32 master weights) and gradient checkpointing (recompute activations during backward to save memory). Distributed training across thousands of GPUs uses 3D parallelism: data parallelism (replicate model, split data), tensor parallelism (split individual layers across GPUs), and pipeline parallelism (split model depth). A GPT-3 pretraining run cost ~$4M in compute at 2020 prices; frontier models now cost $50M–$500M.
concepts.code_example:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Minimal causal LM training loop
def causal_lm_loss(logits, targets):
    """logits: (B, T, V), targets: (B, T)"""
    B, T, V = logits.shape
    # Shift: predict token t from tokens 0..t-1
    return nn.CrossEntropyLoss()(
        logits[:, :-1].reshape(-1, V),
        targets[:, 1:].reshape(-1)
    )

# Tiny demo
torch.manual_seed(0)
vocab_size, seq_len, d_model = 1000, 32, 64
embedding  = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_model)
head       = nn.Linear(d_model, vocab_size, bias=False)
# Weight tying (common in LLMs)
head.weight = embedding.weight

opt = torch.optim.AdamW(
    list(embedding.parameters()) + list(head.parameters()),
    lr=3e-4, weight_decay=0.1
)
for step in range(50):
    tokens = torch.randint(0, vocab_size, (4, seq_len))
    x      = embedding(tokens)
    logits = head(x)
    loss   = causal_lm_loss(logits, tokens)
    opt.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); opt.step()
    if step % 10 == 0:
        print(f"step {step:3d}  loss={loss.item():.4f}")
concepts.plain: Pretraining is the large, expensive first phase of training a language model — exposing it to billions of sentences from the internet and books, learning to predict the next token. The model learns grammar, facts, reasoning patterns, and world knowledge without any task-specific labels. Everything else builds on top of this.

concepts.title: Fine-Tuning
concepts.short: Continuing training a pretrained model on task-specific or domain-specific data to adapt its behaviour.
concepts.body:
Full fine-tuning updates all model parameters on a small labelled dataset. Starting from a pretrained checkpoint dramatically reduces the data and compute required compared to training from scratch — the model already knows language; fine-tuning teaches it the target task or style. Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on instruction-response pairs is how pretrained base LLMs become chat assistants. The training format matters: each example is formatted as a conversation with system/user/assistant turns; the loss is computed only on assistant tokens.

Instruction tuning (FLAN, InstructGPT) fine-tunes on a diverse mixture of tasks formatted as instructions, producing models that generalise to unseen task types zero-shot. Task-specific fine-tuning on a single task (e.g., text classification) achieves higher performance on that task but loses generality. Catastrophic forgetting — the model losing pretrained capabilities when fine-tuned aggressively — is a real risk, addressed by mixing some pretraining data into the fine-tuning set or using regularisation-based methods.

For large models (7B+ parameters), full fine-tuning requires substantial GPU memory. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods — LoRA, prefix tuning, prompt tuning — update a tiny fraction of parameters while keeping most of the model frozen, achieving comparable performance to full fine-tuning at a fraction of the cost. Full fine-tuning of frontier models is now largely replaced by PEFT + RLHF pipelines. Evaluating fine-tuned models requires task-specific benchmarks and human evaluation alongside automated metrics.
concepts.code_example:
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, TrainingArguments, Trainer
from datasets import Dataset
import torch

# Prepare a tiny SFT dataset
examples = [
    {"prompt": "What is gradient descent?",
     "response": "An optimisation algorithm that minimises loss by updating parameters along the negative gradient."},
    {"prompt": "Define overfitting.",
     "response": "When a model memorises training data and fails to generalise to new examples."},
]

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

def format_and_tokenize(ex):
    text = f"Q: {ex['prompt']}\nA: {ex['response']}{tokenizer.eos_token}"
    enc  = tokenizer(text, truncation=True, max_length=128, padding="max_length")
    enc["labels"] = enc["input_ids"].copy()
    return enc

ds = Dataset.from_list(examples).map(format_and_tokenize)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")

args = TrainingArguments(output_dir="/tmp/sft", num_train_epochs=3,
                         per_device_train_batch_size=2, logging_steps=1,
                         report_to="none")
trainer = Trainer(model=model, args=args, train_dataset=ds)
trainer.train()
print("SFT complete")
concepts.plain: Fine-tuning takes a pretrained model and continues training it on a smaller, task-specific dataset. Instead of starting from scratch (which would require billions of examples), you start from a model that already understands language and teach it the specifics of your domain — often achieving great results with just thousands of examples.

concepts.title: RLHF
concepts.short: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback — aligning LLMs with human preferences via a learned reward model.
concepts.body:
RLHF has three stages. First, supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on high-quality human demonstrations produces an initial policy. Second, a reward model (RM) is trained: human annotators compare pairs of model outputs for the same prompt, and the RM learns to score outputs by preference. The RM is typically a smaller LLM with a scalar head, trained with a pairwise ranking loss: log σ(r(x, yᵥ) − r(x, yₗ)) where yᵥ is preferred over yₗ. Third, the SFT model is fine-tuned with PPO to maximise expected RM score, with a KL penalty against the SFT policy to prevent reward hacking.

Reward hacking is the core failure mode: the policy learns to exploit weaknesses in the reward model, producing outputs that score well but are not actually good. The KL penalty constrains how far the policy can drift from the SFT model. Constitutional AI (Anthropic) uses AI feedback (RLAIF) instead of human feedback to scale the preference data collection. DPO (Direct Preference Optimisation) reformulates RLHF as supervised learning directly on preference pairs, eliminating the separate RM training and PPO loop, making it simpler and more stable.

RLHF is the technology behind ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini's assistant behaviours. It teaches models to follow instructions, be helpful, avoid harmful outputs, and match the writing style users prefer. The quality of the preference data is the most important factor: annotators must agree on what 'good' means, have domain expertise for technical content, and apply consistent criteria. Disagreements in annotation are a signal of genuine ambiguity rather than noise to be filtered out.
concepts.code_example:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.nn.functional import logsigmoid

# Reward model training (Bradley-Terry pairwise loss)
class RewardModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, hidden=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.net   = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(hidden, 32), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(32, 1))
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x).squeeze(-1)

torch.manual_seed(0)
rm  = RewardModel(64)
opt = torch.optim.AdamW(rm.parameters(), lr=1e-3)

for step in range(200):
    # Simulate chosen vs rejected response embeddings
    chosen   = torch.randn(16, 64) + 0.5   # slightly better on average
    rejected = torch.randn(16, 64) - 0.5
    r_chosen   = rm(chosen)
    r_rejected = rm(rejected)
    # Pairwise ranking loss: maximise r_chosen - r_rejected
    loss = -logsigmoid(r_chosen - r_rejected).mean()
    opt.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); opt.step()
    if step % 50 == 0:
        acc = (r_chosen > r_rejected).float().mean()
        print(f"step {step:3d}  loss={loss.item():.4f}  acc={acc.item():.3f}")
concepts.plain: RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) is how models are trained to be helpful and safe. Human raters compare different model responses and say which is better. The model is then optimised to produce responses that humans prefer — it's what turns a raw language model into a useful assistant.

concepts.title: Prompt Engineering
concepts.short: Designing input text to reliably elicit desired outputs from a language model without changing its weights.
concepts.body:
Prompt engineering is the practice of crafting, structuring, and iterating on inputs to a language model to reliably obtain desired outputs. Zero-shot prompting relies on the model's pretrained knowledge with no examples. Few-shot prompting includes input-output examples in the prompt that pattern-match the task structure, dramatically improving performance on structured tasks. Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting appends 'Let's think step by step' or includes reasoning traces in examples, eliciting intermediate reasoning that improves accuracy on multi-step problems.

System prompts (in chat models) define the model's persona, constraints, and task context. Clear system prompts reduce hallucination by constraining the space of valid responses. Structural prompting — asking for outputs in specific formats (JSON, lists, XML) — makes responses parseable. Role prompting ('You are an expert in...') shifts the model's response distribution toward that domain. Output constraining (specifying length, format, tone) reduces post-processing needed. Negative prompting ('Do not include...') is less reliable than positive framing ('Include only...').

Common failure modes and fixes: vague instructions produce vague outputs — be explicit about the required output format; model contradicts itself across a long context — use explicit instructions to check consistency; model agrees with incorrect premises — instruct it to first verify assumptions. Prompt injection (malicious user input that overrides system instructions) is a security concern for production systems. Prompt sensitivity — small wording changes producing large output changes — motivates systematic prompt evaluation over representative test sets rather than iterating on single examples.
concepts.code_example:
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Zero-shot
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=256,
    messages=[{"role": "user",
               "content": "Classify sentiment: 'The product broke after a week.'"}]
)
print("Zero-shot:", response.content[0].text)

# Few-shot
few_shot_prompt = """
Classify sentiment as Positive, Negative, or Neutral.

Review: 'Best purchase I have made this year.' -> Positive
Review: 'Stopped working after a day.' -> Negative
Review: 'It arrived on time.' -> Neutral
Review: 'The product broke after a week.' ->"""

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=16,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": few_shot_prompt}]
)
print("Few-shot:", response.content[0].text)

# Chain-of-thought
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=512,
    system="Think step by step before answering.",
    messages=[{"role": "user",
               "content": "If a model achieves 90% accuracy and the baseline is 85%, what is the relative improvement?"}]
)
print("CoT:", response.content[0].text)
concepts.plain: Prompt engineering is the skill of phrasing your instructions to an LLM to get better results. The same underlying model can give wildly different quality answers depending on how the question is framed. Adding context, examples, or step-by-step instructions in your prompt can dramatically improve outputs without changing the model at all.

concepts.title: Few-Shot Learning
concepts.short: Adapting a model to a new task at inference time by including a small number of input-output examples in the prompt.
concepts.body:
Few-shot learning in the context of LLMs refers to in-context learning (ICL): placing k demonstration examples directly in the prompt so the model infers the task pattern without gradient updates. GPT-3 demonstrated that large enough models can perform new tasks from as few as 1-32 examples, a qualitatively different capability from smaller models. The model uses the examples to infer the output format, the mapping from inputs to outputs, and any implicit constraints.

Example selection matters significantly. Random selection from a pool is a reasonable baseline. Retrieval-based selection (find examples most similar to the current query) outperforms random selection on tasks with diverse input types. Example ordering also affects results: models tend to be biased toward the last few examples. Calibration — balancing example labels to match the true prior — prevents the model from defaulting to the majority label in the examples.

Few-shot ICL and fine-tuning are complementary, not alternatives. ICL is preferable when: labelled data is scarce (10-100 examples), the task changes frequently, or model weights cannot be modified. Fine-tuning is preferable when: data is abundant (1000+ examples), task performance is critical, or inference cost from long prompts is prohibitive. The number of tokens consumed by k-shot examples compounds at inference time — 10 examples of 200 tokens each adds 2000 tokens to every request, which at high volume represents significant cost.
concepts.code_example:
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# In-context learning: entity extraction
examples = [
    ("Apple acquired Intel's smartphone modem business.",
     '{"acquirer": "Apple", "target": "Intel smartphone modem business"}'),
    ("Microsoft bought Activision Blizzard for $69 billion.",
     '{"acquirer": "Microsoft", "target": "Activision Blizzard"}'),
]

def build_prompt(query, examples):
    shots = "\n".join(
        f"Text: {text}\nJSON: {label}" for text, label in examples
    )
    return f"{shots}\nText: {query}\nJSON:"

query = "Amazon purchased MGM Studios in a deal worth $8.45 billion."
prompt = build_prompt(query, examples)

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=100,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
)
print("Extracted:", response.content[0].text)

# Effect of k on performance (conceptual)
for k in [0, 1, 3]:
    shots = examples[:k]
    print(f"k={k}: {len(build_prompt(query, shots))} prompt chars")
concepts.plain: Few-shot learning means giving a model a few examples of a task in the prompt, without any retraining. 'Translate English to French: cat→chat, dog→chien, horse→?' The model picks up the pattern from the examples you gave it. A single model can do dozens of tasks just by changing the examples in the prompt.

concepts.title: RAG
concepts.short: Retrieval-Augmented Generation — grounding LLM responses by injecting retrieved documents into the prompt.
concepts.body:
RAG combines a retrieval system with a generative LLM. Given a query, the retriever fetches relevant documents from a knowledge base; these are prepended to the LLM prompt so the model generates its response grounded in retrieved evidence. This solves two key limitations of standalone LLMs: knowledge cutoff (the model knows nothing after its training data) and hallucination (the model generates plausible but false claims for facts not strongly reinforced during training).

The retriever is typically a dense retrieval system: documents are chunked, embedded with an embedding model, and indexed in a vector database. At query time, the query is embedded with the same model and ANN search retrieves the top-k most similar chunks. Sparse retrieval (BM25 keyword search) captures exact-match signals that dense retrieval misses; hybrid search combining both outperforms either alone on most benchmarks. Reranking (a smaller cross-encoder model that scores (query, document) pairs) improves precision by reordering the top-k before passing to the LLM.

RAG vs fine-tuning is a common architectural decision. RAG is better for: dynamic knowledge (updated without retraining), verifiable claims (sources can be cited), privacy-sensitive data (keep out of training), and large knowledge bases. Fine-tuning is better for: changing the model's style or format, internalising stable knowledge at lower inference cost, and tasks requiring deep integration of knowledge with reasoning. Many production systems use both: fine-tune for format/behaviour, RAG for up-to-date knowledge retrieval.
concepts.code_example:
# pip install chromadb sentence-transformers anthropic
import chromadb
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import anthropic

# Build knowledge base
docs = [
    "Transformers use self-attention to model sequence dependencies in parallel.",
    "BERT is pretrained with masked language modelling on bidirectional context.",
    "GPT uses causal (left-to-right) attention for autoregressive generation.",
    "LoRA inserts low-rank matrices into frozen model layers for efficient fine-tuning.",
]

emb_model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
client_db  = chromadb.Client()
col        = client_db.create_collection("kb")
vecs       = emb_model.encode(docs).tolist()
col.add(documents=docs, embeddings=vecs, ids=[str(i) for i in range(len(docs))])

def rag_query(question, top_k=2):
    q_vec   = emb_model.encode([question]).tolist()
    results = col.query(query_embeddings=q_vec, n_results=top_k)
    context = "\n".join(results["documents"][0])
    prompt  = f"Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}\nAnswer:"
    client  = anthropic.Anthropic()
    resp    = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=200,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return resp.content[0].text

print(rag_query("How does BERT differ from GPT?"))
concepts.plain: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) gives an LLM access to a knowledge base at query time. Instead of relying only on what it learned during training (which might be outdated or incomplete), it searches for relevant documents and includes them in the prompt. The result: grounded, up-to-date answers with less hallucination.

concepts.title: Advanced RAG
concepts.short: Advanced RAG patterns covering chunking strategies, hybrid search, reranking, and query transformation.
concepts.body:
Naive RAG (embed chunks, retrieve by cosine similarity, stuff into prompt) breaks down in production. Advanced RAG addresses four main failure modes. First, chunking: fixed-size chunks split mid-sentence, losing context. Semantic chunking (split at sentence boundaries respecting semantic coherence), hierarchical chunking (store both sentence-level and paragraph-level chunks), and parent-child retrieval (retrieve small chunks, expand to their containing paragraph) all improve context quality. Chunk overlap mitigates boundary effects.

Second, retrieval quality: dense retrieval misses keyword-critical queries; sparse retrieval (BM25) misses semantic queries. Hybrid search with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) combines both retrieval signals. HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) generates a hypothetical answer to the query, embeds it, and retrieves documents similar to that hypothetical — remarkably effective because the hypothetical is in the same distribution as the corpus. Query rewriting (having the LLM rephrase the query for better retrieval) improves recall.

Third, context utilisation: LLMs suffer from the 'lost-in-the-middle' phenomenon — information at the start and end of context is better utilised than information in the middle. Reranking improves the ordering of chunks. Contextual compression (LLM extracts only the relevant sentence from each retrieved document) reduces noise. Fourth, generation faithfulness: constrained generation (instruct the model to cite sources), self-consistency checks, and factual verification with a second LLM call reduce hallucination. Evaluation with RAGAS (faithfulness, answer relevancy, context precision/recall) quantifies end-to-end quality.
concepts.plain: Retrieval-Augmented Generation is the full system: a search engine finds relevant documents from a knowledge base; an LLM reads them and generates an answer. The retrieval grounds the LLM in real evidence. It's the standard pattern for building Q&A systems over private or frequently-updated data.

concepts.title: Function Calling
concepts.short: A model capability to emit structured tool invocations that calling code executes on the model's behalf.
concepts.body:
Function calling (tool use) allows LLMs to request execution of external functions in a structured, parseable format. The developer defines tools as JSON schemas describing function names, parameters, and types. The model decides when to call a tool, generates a valid JSON invocation, and pauses. The application executes the function and returns the result. The model incorporates the result and either calls another tool or generates a final response. This closes the loop between language understanding and real-world actions.

The tool schema is part of the system prompt and consumed as tokens. Well-defined schemas with clear descriptions and parameter documentation dramatically improve tool selection accuracy. Models fine-tuned on function calling data (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) reliably select the right tool and generate valid parameter values. Parallel tool calling — the model emitting multiple tool calls simultaneously when they are independent — reduces round-trip latency in multi-tool workflows.

Error handling is non-trivial: tool calls may fail (network errors, invalid parameters), return unexpected formats, or require retry logic. The application layer must handle these cases and return informative error messages back to the model so it can recover. Streaming function calls allows the UI to show progress before all tool results are available. Security considerations: tools should be sandboxed, parameters validated before execution, and capabilities restricted to the minimum necessary — an LLM with unrestricted shell access is a significant attack surface if the model is exposed to adversarial inputs.
concepts.plain: Function calling lets an LLM produce a structured request to trigger a real action — search the web, run a database query, call an API — rather than just describing in text what it would do. The model outputs a function name and arguments in a structured format that your code can execute directly.

concepts.title: Agents
concepts.short: LLM-powered systems that autonomously plan and execute multi-step tasks by iteratively calling tools.
concepts.body:
An agent is an LLM embedded in a loop: observe state → reason → act → observe result → repeat until the task is complete. The ReAct pattern (Reason + Act) structures each iteration as: Thought (the model's internal reasoning), Action (a tool call), Observation (the tool result). This interleaving of reasoning and acting produces substantially better task completion than single-pass generation. The agent terminates when it decides it has enough information or reaches a maximum step limit.

Memory is a key design axis. In-context memory (all previous steps in the prompt) is simple but grows with every step, eventually exhausting the context window. Summarisation of older steps compresses history. External memory (a database of past interactions, retrieved by similarity) scales to unlimited history. Working memory (a scratchpad the agent explicitly writes to and reads from) enables structured state tracking for complex tasks.

Agent failure modes: getting stuck in loops (calling the same tool repeatedly without progress), incorrect tool use (wrong parameters, misinterpreting results), planning failures (reasonable-seeming steps that don't achieve the goal), and compound errors (early mistakes compounding through subsequent steps). Defensive design: max step limits, tool call validation, human-in-the-loop checkpoints for irreversible actions, and structured prompts that explicitly ask the agent to verify progress before continuing. Multi-agent systems (planner agent + specialist subagents) decompose complex tasks but add coordination complexity.
concepts.code_example:
import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Minimal ReAct agent
tools = [
    {"name": "calculator", "description": "Evaluate a math expression.",
     "input_schema": {"type": "object",
                      "properties": {"expression": {"type": "string"}},
                      "required": ["expression"]}},
    {"name": "done", "description": "Signal task complete with final answer.",
     "input_schema": {"type": "object",
                      "properties": {"answer": {"type": "string"}},
                      "required": ["answer"]}},
]

def run_tool(name, inputs):
    if name == "calculator":
        try:    return str(eval(inputs["expression"]))
        except: return "Error"
    return inputs.get("answer", "")

def run_agent(task, max_steps=6):
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": task}]
    for step in range(max_steps):
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=512,
            tools=tools, messages=messages
        )
        if resp.stop_reason == "end_turn": break
        tool = next((b for b in resp.content if b.type == "tool_use"), None)
        if not tool: break
        result = run_tool(tool.name, tool.input)
        print(f"[step {step+1}] {tool.name}({tool.input}) -> {result}")
        if tool.name == "done": return result
        messages += [{"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content},
                     {"role": "user",      "content": [{"type": "tool_result",
                                                        "tool_use_id": tool.id,
                                                        "content": result}]}]

run_agent("What is (17 * 23) + (144 / 12)?")
concepts.plain: An AI agent is an LLM that can take actions and repeat them in a loop until a goal is achieved. Instead of answering one question, it can use tools, check the results, decide what to do next, and keep going. This turns a conversational model into something that can complete multi-step, real-world tasks.

concepts.title: Tool Use
concepts.short: The broader capability of LLMs to invoke external systems — APIs, databases, browsers — to extend beyond text generation.
concepts.body:
Tool use encompasses the full ecosystem of capabilities an LLM can access beyond its weights: web search, code execution, file I/O, database queries, API calls, browser automation, image generation, and more. The tool abstraction is the same regardless of the underlying capability: a schema describes the tool, the model emits a structured invocation, the application executes it and returns results. What varies is the complexity, latency, and side-effect risk of each tool.

Tool design principles: tools should do one thing well (single responsibility), accept and return structured data (not raw text), be idempotent where possible (safe to retry), and return informative error messages the LLM can act on. Overly broad tools (a generic shell command tool) give the model too much surface area and create security risks. Overly narrow tools produce verbose tool-calling traces for simple tasks. The right granularity is discovered empirically — start broad, decompose as complexity demands.

MCP (Model Context Protocol, Anthropic) is an open standard for exposing tools as servers that models can connect to, enabling a marketplace of reusable tool integrations. This decouples tool development from model deployment: a single MCP server for GitHub tools can be used by any MCP-compatible model. Computer use extends tool use to direct GUI interaction: the model takes screenshots, identifies UI elements, and emits mouse/keyboard actions, enabling automation of any software without API integration. This is powerful but slow and brittle compared to structured API tools.
concepts.plain: Tool use is the ability for an agent to reach outside the language model — to call a calculator, search the web, run code, or query a database. Without tools, the model can only work with what's in its context. With tools, it can retrieve fresh information, perform precise computations, and take actions in the world.

concepts.title: Context Window
concepts.short: The maximum number of tokens an LLM can attend to in a single forward pass — its working memory.
concepts.body:
The context window is the total token budget for a single model call: system prompt + conversation history + retrieved documents + tool call results + the model's response all count against it. Early models (GPT-3) had 4K token windows; GPT-4-turbo and Claude 3 extended to 128K; Gemini 1.5 demonstrated 1M tokens. The quadratic cost of attention (O(n²) in sequence length) means naive transformers become prohibitively expensive at very long contexts; Flash Attention and KV-cache management make long contexts practical.

The KV-cache stores the key and value projections for every token in the context so they do not need to be recomputed for each new generated token. For a 100K context, the KV-cache can require gigabytes of GPU memory. Cache-efficient serving groups requests with shared prefixes (same system prompt) and reuses the cached KV states across requests — this is why Anthropic's prompt caching API can save 90%+ of time-to-first-token for long system prompts.

Long context introduces the 'lost-in-the-middle' problem: models exhibit recency and primacy bias, performing worse on information buried in the middle of a long context. Practical strategies: put the most important content at the start or end; use RAG to retrieve only relevant chunks rather than stuffing everything; summarise or compress older conversation turns. Context window size is not a free lunch — more context means higher latency, higher cost, and potentially lower accuracy on the truly relevant information due to distraction from irrelevant content.
concepts.code_example:
import anthropic
import tiktoken

# Count tokens before sending to avoid context limit errors
def count_tokens(text: str, model: str = "cl100k_base") -> int:
    enc = tiktoken.get_encoding(model)
    return len(enc.encode(text))

CONTEXT_LIMIT = 200_000  # Claude claude-sonnet-4-6

def build_prompt_within_limit(system, history, documents, query,
                               reserve_output=2000):
    available = CONTEXT_LIMIT - reserve_output
    parts     = [system, query]
    base_toks = sum(count_tokens(p) for p in parts)
    # Greedily add history turns newest-first
    history_text = ""
    for turn in reversed(history):
        t = f"User: {turn['user']}\nAssistant: {turn['assistant']}\n"
        if base_toks + count_tokens(history_text + t) < available * 0.4:
            history_text = t + history_text
    # Fill remaining budget with documents
    doc_text = ""
    for doc in documents:
        if base_toks + count_tokens(history_text + doc_text + doc) < available:
            doc_text += doc + "\n"
    return system, history_text + doc_text + query

system, prompt = build_prompt_within_limit(
    system="You are a helpful assistant.",
    history=[{"user": "What is attention?", "assistant": "..."}],
    documents=["Attention paper: ..."],
    query="Summarise the key ideas."
)
print(f"Prompt tokens: {count_tokens(prompt)}")
concepts.plain: The context window is everything the model can see at once — the prompt, the conversation history, any documents you've added. Information outside the context window is completely invisible to the model. As tasks get longer, managing what fits in the context window becomes one of the central engineering challenges.

concepts.title: Quantization
concepts.short: Reducing model weight precision from 32-bit floats to lower-bit formats to cut memory and speed up inference.
concepts.body:
A 7B parameter model in float32 requires 28GB of memory; in float16/bfloat16, 14GB; in int8, 7GB; in int4, 3.5GB. Quantization maps continuous floating-point weights to a discrete set of values using a scale factor and zero-point. Post-training quantization (PTQ) applies to a trained model without any further gradient updates. Activation-aware quantization (AWQ, GPTQ) calibrates quantization ranges using a small dataset to minimise the impact on activation distributions — critical because uniform quantization applied to weight outliers destroys model quality.

GGUF (the format used by llama.cpp) enables running quantized models on CPU with optional GPU offloading, making 7B–13B models viable on consumer hardware. Mixed precision quantization assigns different bit widths to different layers — attention layers and the first/last layers are quantization-sensitive, while FFN middle layers tolerate aggressive quantization. Q4_K_M and Q5_K_M are popular GGUF presets offering good quality-size tradeoffs. KV-cache quantization reduces the memory footprint of long-context inference independently of weight quantization.

bitsandbytes provides drop-in 8-bit and 4-bit quantization for HuggingFace models. 4-bit NF4 (NormalFloat4) quantization, used in QLoRA, is specifically designed for normally distributed weights. SpQR and QuIP# push towards extreme 2-bit quantization with acceptable quality. The key tradeoff is quality vs memory: int8 typically loses <1% on benchmarks; int4 loses 2-5%; further compression degrades faster. For throughput-optimised serving, FP8 (natively supported on H100 GPUs) provides almost lossless quantization with 2x memory reduction versus fp16.
concepts.code_example:
# pip install bitsandbytes transformers accelerate
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig
import torch

quant_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,   # nested quantization
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",        # NormalFloat4
)

# Load a model in 4-bit (requires GPU)
# model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
#     "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf",
#     quantization_config=quant_config,
#     device_map="auto",
# )

# Quantization effect demo (CPU, no GPU needed)
import numpy as np

def quantize_int8(w: np.ndarray):
    scale  = np.abs(w).max() / 127
    q      = np.clip(np.round(w / scale), -128, 127).astype(np.int8)
    return q, scale

def dequantize(q, scale):
    return q.astype(np.float32) * scale

np.random.seed(0)
w = np.random.randn(4, 4).astype(np.float32)
q, scale = quantize_int8(w)
w_hat    = dequantize(q, scale)
print("Original:\n",   w.round(4))
print("Reconstructed:", w_hat.round(4))
print(f"Max error: {np.abs(w - w_hat).max():.6f}")
print(f"Memory: fp32={w.nbytes}B  int8={q.nbytes}B  ({q.nbytes/w.nbytes:.0%})")
concepts.plain: Quantisation reduces the precision of a model's numbers — from 32-bit floating point to 8-bit or 4-bit integers — making the model much smaller and faster. A model that normally requires a powerful server can become small enough to run on a laptop. There's usually a small quality tradeoff, but often it's worth it.

concepts.title: LoRA
concepts.short: Low-Rank Adaptation — fine-tuning LLMs by training only small low-rank matrices injected into frozen layers.
concepts.body:
LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) freezes the pretrained weights W₀ and injects trainable low-rank decompositions ΔW = BA where B ∈ ℝ^(d×r), A ∈ ℝ^(r×k), and rank r ≪ min(d,k). The forward pass becomes h = W₀x + (BA)x/√r. Only A and B are trained — for a 7B parameter model targeting all attention projection matrices with rank 8, LoRA trains ~0.1% of parameters while achieving performance close to full fine-tuning. The trained adapter is a small file (10-100MB) that can be shared, swapped, and merged into the base weights.

The rank r and alpha (α, a scaling factor typically set to r or 2r) are the main hyperparameters. Low rank (r=4,8) suffices for style and format adaptation; higher rank (r=16,32,64) is needed for complex skill learning. Target modules typically include all query, key, value, and output projection matrices in attention; sometimes the FFN linear layers too. RSLoRA scales the adapter by 1/√r instead of 1/r, improving stability at higher ranks. DoRA (Weight-Decomposed LoRA) decomposes weights into magnitude and direction components, adapting them separately.

QLoRA (Quantized LoRA) combines 4-bit NF4 quantization of the base model with LoRA adapters in bf16, enabling fine-tuning of 65B parameter models on a single 48GB GPU. The gradient flows through the dequantization operation and into the LoRA matrices — the base model weights never update. This democratised LLM fine-tuning, making it accessible on a single high-end consumer GPU (RTX 4090). After training, LoRA adapters can be merged back into the base weights (W_merged = W₀ + BA) for zero-overhead inference.
concepts.code_example:
# pip install peft transformers
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("gpt2")
model     = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("gpt2")

lora_config = LoraConfig(
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM,
    r=8,              # rank
    lora_alpha=16,    # scaling = alpha/r
    target_modules=["c_attn"],  # GPT-2 attention projection
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none",
)

model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()

# Check trainable vs total
total    = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
trainable = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
print(f"Trainable: {trainable:,}  /  Total: {total:,}  "
      f"({100*trainable/total:.2f}%)")

# Training proceeds normally — only LoRA matrices update
opt = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=2e-4)
tokens = tokenizer("Fine-tuning with LoRA is efficient.", return_tensors="pt")
loss   = model(**tokens, labels=tokens["input_ids"]).loss
opt.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); opt.step()
print(f"Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")
concepts.plain: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a way to fine-tune a large model cheaply. Instead of updating all billions of parameters, it adds tiny adapter layers alongside the original weights and only trains those. This requires a fraction of the GPU memory and compute of full fine-tuning while producing surprisingly competitive results.

concepts.title: Data Pipeline
concepts.short: The automated flow of data from raw sources through processing steps to a form ready for ML use.
concepts.body:
A data pipeline moves data from origin (databases, event streams, files, APIs) through a series of transformations (cleaning, joining, aggregating, encoding) to a destination (feature store, training dataset, serving store). Reliability is the primary concern: pipelines must handle source failures, schema changes, duplicate records, and late-arriving data without corrupting downstream consumers. Idempotency — the ability to safely re-run a pipeline and get the same result — is a critical design property.

ETL (Extract-Transform-Load) stages data into a warehouse after transformation; ELT loads raw data first and transforms in the warehouse using SQL. ELT is dominant in modern data stacks because warehouse compute is cheap and raw data preservation enables schema-on-read flexibility. Orchestration tools (Apache Airflow, Prefect, Dagster) manage task dependencies, scheduling, retries, and alerting. Each task in a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) is a unit of work with explicit upstream dependencies.

ML-specific pipeline concerns: point-in-time correctness for training data (features computed using only information available before each label's timestamp), data versioning (knowing exactly which data trained a given model), and schema enforcement (preventing silent type changes from corrupting feature values). Data quality checks — row count expectations, null rate thresholds, distribution bounds — should be gates in the pipeline that fail loudly rather than passing bad data silently downstream.
concepts.code_example:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

@dataclass
class PipelineStage:
    name: str
    fn:   Callable

class DataPipeline:
    def __init__(self): self.stages: list[PipelineStage] = []
    def add(self, name, fn): self.stages.append(PipelineStage(name, fn))
    def run(self, df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
        for stage in self.stages:
            before = len(df)
            df     = stage.fn(df)
            print(f"  [{stage.name}] rows: {before} -> {len(df)}")
        return df

np.random.seed(0)
raw = pd.DataFrame({
    "user_id": [1,2,3,None,2,1],
    "amount":  [10.0, -5.0, 200.0, 15.0, None, 8.0],
    "ts":      pd.date_range("2024-01-01", periods=6, freq="h"),
})

pipe = DataPipeline()
pipe.add("drop_null_user",  lambda df: df.dropna(subset=["user_id"]))
pipe.add("drop_neg_amount", lambda df: df[df["amount"] > 0])
pipe.add("fill_amount",     lambda df: df.assign(amount=df["amount"].fillna(df["amount"].median())))
pipe.add("add_hour",        lambda df: df.assign(hour=df["ts"].dt.hour))

print("Running pipeline:")
result = pipe.run(raw)
print(result)
concepts.plain: A data pipeline is the automated system that collects raw data, cleans and transforms it, and delivers it where it needs to go — a feature store, a training job, a dashboard. Without it, every data preparation step is manual and error-prone. Pipelines make the whole process repeatable and reliable.

concepts.title: Streaming vs Batch Processing
concepts.short: The choice between processing data continuously as it arrives versus in scheduled bulk jobs.
concepts.body:
Batch processing collects data over a period, then processes it all at once. Simple, efficient, easy to reason about — a nightly Spark job that computes user features from the past 30 days is a canonical example. The tradeoff is latency: features are at most 24 hours stale. Batch is appropriate when models don't require real-time features, when the compute is expensive enough to amortise over many records, or when correctness requires seeing the full dataset (sorting, global statistics).

Stream processing handles events as they arrive, maintaining running state and emitting updated results continuously. Apache Kafka is the dominant message bus; Apache Flink and Spark Streaming compute over event streams. Stream processing enables real-time features (fraud score using the last 10 transactions in the past 5 minutes) but adds complexity: out-of-order events, exactly-once semantics, state management, and watermarking (deciding when to consider a time window complete despite late arrivals). Lambda architecture runs both batch and streaming, using batch results as ground truth that eventually overwrites streaming approximations.

The practical decision tree: if feature staleness > 1 hour is acceptable, batch is simpler. If features need to reflect events from the past minutes, streaming is required. Most production ML systems are hybrid: a daily batch job computes stable long-window features (7-day purchase count); a streaming pipeline computes volatile short-window features (clicks in the last 5 minutes); both feed the feature store. The streaming layer is more expensive to build and operate — justify it with a concrete business requirement before adding the complexity.
concepts.code_example:
import time
from collections import defaultdict, deque
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Event:
    user_id: int
    amount:  float
    ts:      float  # unix timestamp

class StreamingFeatureEngine:
    """Sliding-window feature computation over an event stream."""
    def __init__(self, window_seconds=300):
        self.window  = window_seconds
        self.events  = defaultdict(deque)  # user_id -> deque of events

    def process(self, event: Event) -> dict:
        buf = self.events[event.user_id]
        buf.append(event)
        cutoff = event.ts - self.window
        while buf and buf[0].ts < cutoff:
            buf.popleft()
        amounts = [e.amount for e in buf]
        return {
            "user_id":       event.user_id,
            "count_5min":    len(amounts),
            "sum_5min":      sum(amounts),
            "max_5min":      max(amounts) if amounts else 0,
        }

engine = StreamingFeatureEngine(window_seconds=300)
now    = time.time()
events = [
    Event(1, 50.0,  now - 400),
    Event(1, 20.0,  now - 100),
    Event(1, 200.0, now - 10),
    Event(2, 15.0,  now - 50),
]
for e in events:
    features = engine.process(e)
    print(features)
concepts.plain: Batch processing handles large chunks of data at scheduled intervals — like a nightly job that processes all of yesterday's transactions. Stream processing handles each event in real time as it arrives. Batch is simpler and cheaper; stream is faster but more complex. The right choice depends on how fresh your data needs to be.

concepts.title: Feature Engineering
concepts.short: Transforming raw data into informative numerical representations that improve model learning.
concepts.body:
Feature engineering is the process of using domain knowledge to create input representations that make patterns learnable by the chosen model. A timestamp alone is not useful; extracting hour-of-day, day-of-week, is-weekend, days-since-last-purchase transforms it into informative signals. Raw transaction amounts benefit from log transformation to reduce skew; cyclical features (hour, month) benefit from sine/cosine encoding to preserve circular continuity.

Categorical encoding is a major source of feature engineering decisions. One-hot encoding is appropriate for low-cardinality nominals; it is wasteful for high-cardinality features (user IDs, ZIP codes). Target encoding (replacing a category with the mean target value of that category in the training set) is powerful but prone to leakage without proper cross-fold estimation. Embedding layers (for neural models) learn dense representations for high-cardinality categoricals end-to-end. Binary encoding, frequency encoding, and hashing are practical alternatives for tree models.

Interaction features (product of two columns) capture non-additive relationships that linear models cannot learn. Lag features (value at time t-1, t-7) are essential for time series. Ratio features (amount / median-amount-for-user) normalise absolute values relative to baseline. Polynomial features expand linear models to quadratic decision boundaries. The best features usually come from domain expertise: a fraud analyst knows that the combination of 'new device' + 'large foreign transaction' is a stronger signal than either alone. Automated feature engineering (featuretools, deep feature synthesis) can discover interaction features at scale.
concepts.code_example:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder

np.random.seed(0)
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "ts":       pd.date_range("2024-01-01", periods=100, freq="6h"),
    "amount":   np.random.exponential(50, 100),
    "category": np.random.choice(["groceries","travel","food","tech"], 100),
    "user_id":  np.random.randint(1, 11, 100),
})

# Temporal features
df["hour"]       = df["ts"].dt.hour
df["dow"]        = df["ts"].dt.dayofweek
df["is_weekend"] = (df["dow"] >= 5).astype(int)
# Cyclical encoding of hour
df["hour_sin"]   = np.sin(2 * np.pi * df["hour"] / 24)
df["hour_cos"]   = np.cos(2 * np.pi * df["hour"] / 24)

# Log-transform skewed numeric
df["log_amount"] = np.log1p(df["amount"])

# Target encoding (train-only; use cross-fold in practice)
cat_mean = df.groupby("category")["amount"].mean()
df["cat_amount_mean"] = df["category"].map(cat_mean)

# User-relative amount
user_mean = df.groupby("user_id")["amount"].transform("mean")
df["amount_vs_user_mean"] = df["amount"] / (user_mean + 1e-9)

print(df[["hour_sin","hour_cos","log_amount","cat_amount_mean","amount_vs_user_mean"]].head())
concepts.plain: Feature engineering is the process of extracting the most useful signals from raw data before feeding it to a model. Raw data is rarely model-ready — dates need to become day-of-week or time-since-last-event, categories need encoding, numerical values need scaling. Good features often matter more than the choice of model.

concepts.title: Online vs Offline Inference
concepts.short: Choosing between computing predictions at request time (online) or precomputing them in bulk (offline).
concepts.body:
Online inference (real-time) computes predictions synchronously during each request. The model runs in a serving stack, features are fetched from the online feature store, and a response is returned within tens of milliseconds. This is required when predictions depend on real-time context (the user's current session, the current inventory level) or when the prediction space is too large to precompute exhaustively (any user × any item from a catalogue of millions).

Offline inference (batch prediction) precomputes predictions for a known population ahead of time and stores them for lookup. A churn model scores all 10M users every night; results sit in a database; when a user logs in, their pre-computed score is retrieved with a database lookup. This decouples model inference from request latency — the model can be arbitrarily slow because it runs offline. It is appropriate when the prediction entity set is bounded, predictions are relatively stable (daily or hourly refresh is sufficient), and the marginal cost of computing predictions for entities that are never queried is acceptable.

Hybrid approaches are common. A recommendation system uses offline batch scoring to generate a broad candidate set per user (top-1000 items, refreshed daily), then online ranking to reorder the candidates in real time using fresh session features. The offline phase handles the expensive retrieval; the online phase handles the latency-sensitive personalisation. Near-line inference — an asynchronous queue that computes predictions shortly after triggering events (user login, new item added) — is a middle ground between the two extremes.
concepts.code_example:
import time
import random
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

# Simulating offline batch prediction
class BatchPredictor:
    def __init__(self, model_fn, batch_size=256):
        self.model_fn   = model_fn
        self.batch_size = batch_size

    def score_all(self, user_ids: list) -> dict:
        scores = {}
        for i in range(0, len(user_ids), self.batch_size):
            batch = user_ids[i:i+self.batch_size]
            # Simulate model inference
            preds = self.model_fn(batch)
            scores.update(zip(batch, preds))
        return scores

def fake_model(user_ids):
    time.sleep(0.001)  # batch latency
    return [random.random() for _ in user_ids]

user_ids = list(range(10_000))
t0       = time.time()
batcher  = BatchPredictor(fake_model, batch_size=256)
all_scores = batcher.score_all(user_ids)
print(f"Offline: scored {len(all_scores)} users in {time.time()-t0:.2f}s")

# Online lookup — sub-millisecond
t1 = time.time()
for uid in random.sample(user_ids, 100):
    _ = all_scores[uid]  # dict lookup
print(f"Online lookup: 100 users in {(time.time()-t1)*1000:.2f}ms")
concepts.plain: Offline inference computes predictions in advance for a large batch of inputs and stores them (like pre-computing tomorrow's product recommendations tonight). Online inference computes predictions in real time when a request arrives. Offline is cheaper and faster to serve; online is fresher but more expensive to run.

concepts.title: Latency vs Throughput
concepts.short: The fundamental tension between how fast a single prediction takes versus how many predictions can be served per second.
concepts.body:
Latency is the time from receiving a request to returning a response — typically measured as p50, p95, and p99 (the median, 95th, and 99th percentile). p99 latency is what the slowest 1% of users experience; high p99 often reveals resource contention, garbage collection pauses, or cold-start effects. Throughput is requests per second the system can sustain. Little's Law: throughput = concurrency / mean latency. If mean latency is 10ms and the system handles 50 concurrent requests, throughput is 5000 req/s.

Batching is the primary lever for trading latency for throughput on GPU inference. A single GPU forward pass for batch size 1 takes nearly the same time as batch size 32 — the memory bandwidth bottleneck means the GPU is underutilised at batch size 1. Dynamic batching in serving frameworks (Triton, TorchServe) accumulates requests arriving within a short window (1-5ms) and processes them together. This increases individual request latency by up to 5ms but can improve throughput by 10-30x. The sweet spot depends on the SLO: if the SLO is 50ms and inference takes 20ms, there is 30ms to batch without violating the SLO.

Hardware choices also define the tradeoff. A single high-end GPU (H100) has high throughput per dollar for large batches. Many smaller inference-optimised accelerators (Inferentia, TPU v5e) provide better cost-per-request at lower latency. CPU inference (ONNX, llama.cpp) has higher per-request latency but can be horizontally scaled cheaply. For LLMs, the distinction between time-to-first-token (TTFT, user-perceived latency) and tokens-per-second (throughput) creates a two-dimensional SLO — speculative decoding and continuous batching are techniques for improving both simultaneously.
concepts.plain: Latency is how long one request takes. Throughput is how many requests you can handle per second. Optimising for one often hurts the other — batching requests together increases throughput but adds latency to each individual request. Production ML systems are full of these tradeoffs.

concepts.title: Model Versioning
concepts.short: Tracking multiple trained model versions to enable rollback, reproducibility, and safe deployment.
concepts.body:
Model versioning tracks every trained model artifact alongside the metadata needed to reproduce and compare it: training data snapshot, code commit, hyperparameters, evaluation metrics, and environment dependencies. Without versioning, a degraded deployment cannot be rolled back reliably (what exactly was the previous model?), debugging production issues is difficult (what changed between this week and last week?), and regulatory audits are impossible (what model made this decision for this user on this date?).

Semantic versioning (major.minor.patch) works for model families but not for individual trained checkpoints — models should be identified by a hash of their training lineage, not a human-assigned number. DVC (Data Version Control) extends git to track large binary artifacts (model weights, datasets) using content-addressed storage with pointers in the git repo. This enables `git checkout` to restore both code and data to any historical state. Model registries (MLflow, SageMaker) provide a higher-level UI for managing the model lifecycle.

Practical rollback requires more than just storing old weights. The serving infrastructure must be able to deploy the old model version quickly (minutes, not hours). The preprocessing and feature engineering code that transforms raw inputs before the model must also be versioned and compatible — a model trained on v2 features cannot be rolled back while the feature pipeline runs v3 logic. Pinning the entire inference stack (model + featuriser + postprocessor) as a versioned unit prevents this compatibility problem.
concepts.code_example:
import hashlib
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from datetime import datetime
from pathlib import Path

@dataclass
class ModelVersion:
    model_id:    str
    created_at:  str
    metrics:     dict
    params:      dict
    data_hash:   str
    code_commit: str
    stage:       str = "staging"  # staging | production | archived

    def lineage_hash(self) -> str:
        payload = json.dumps(
            {"params": self.params, "data_hash": self.data_hash,
             "code_commit": self.code_commit}, sort_keys=True)
        return hashlib.sha256(payload.encode()).hexdigest()[:12]

class ModelRegistry:
    def __init__(self): self.versions: dict[str, ModelVersion] = {}
    def register(self, mv: ModelVersion): self.versions[mv.model_id] = mv
    def promote(self, model_id):
        for mv in self.versions.values():
            if mv.stage == "production": mv.stage = "archived"
        self.versions[model_id].stage = "production"
    def production(self): return next((v for v in self.versions.values() if v.stage=="production"), None)

reg = ModelRegistry()
for i, auc in enumerate([0.81, 0.83, 0.85]):
    mv = ModelVersion(f"rf-v{i}", datetime.now().isoformat(),
                      {"auc": auc}, {"n_estimators": 100+i*50},
                      f"datahash_{i}", f"commit_{i}")
    reg.register(mv)
    print(f"Registered {mv.model_id}  lineage={mv.lineage_hash()}")
reg.promote("rf-v2")
print(f"Production: {reg.production().model_id}")
concepts.plain: Model versioning is tracking every saved version of a model — what data trained it, what code produced it, what its performance was. When a newly deployed model starts making worse predictions, you need versioning to know exactly what changed and to roll back safely.

concepts.title: Canary Deployment
concepts.short: A deployment strategy that routes a small traffic slice to a new model version before full rollout.
concepts.body:
Canary deployment routes a small fraction (1-5%) of live traffic to the new model version while the majority continues to hit the production model. This exposes the new model to real users and real edge cases — the most reliable test — while limiting blast radius. The canary is monitored for anomalies: error rate spikes, latency regressions, output distribution shifts, or business metric degradation. If metrics are healthy, the canary percentage is gradually increased; if not, the canary is rolled back without the majority of users being affected.

Canary differs from A/B testing in intent. A/B tests are designed to measure effect size with statistical confidence — they run for fixed durations, are analysed post-hoc, and require pre-specified sample sizes. Canaries are safety checks — they run until the team is confident the new version is not broken, then expand. A/B tests answer 'Is B better than A?'; canaries answer 'Is B safe to deploy?'. Many production deployments use both: canary to verify safety, then an A/B test to confirm the expected business metric lift.

Implementation: load balancers (Nginx, Envoy, Istio) support percentage-based traffic splitting by weight. Kubernetes with Argo Rollouts or Flagger automates canary analysis and progressive rollout. The key operational requirement is that both model versions must be running simultaneously, which doubles serving infrastructure cost during the rollout window. Sticky sessions (same user always hits the same version) are important for multi-turn interactions or experiments where within-session consistency matters.
concepts.code_example:
import random
import time
from collections import defaultdict

class CanaryRouter:
    def __init__(self, canary_pct=0.05, sticky=True):
        self.canary_pct  = canary_pct
        self.sticky      = sticky
        self._user_map   = {}  # sticky assignment cache
        self.metrics     = defaultdict(lambda: {"requests":0,"errors":0,"latency":[]})

    def route(self, user_id: str) -> str:
        if self.sticky:
            if user_id not in self._user_map:
                self._user_map[user_id] = "canary" if random.random() < self.canary_pct else "production"
            return self._user_map[user_id]
        return "canary" if random.random() < self.canary_pct else "production"

    def record(self, version, latency_ms, error=False):
        m = self.metrics[version]
        m["requests"] += 1
        m["latency"].append(latency_ms)
        if error: m["errors"] += 1

    def health_check(self):
        for ver, m in self.metrics.items():
            if not m["requests"]: continue
            err_rate = m["errors"] / m["requests"]
            p99      = sorted(m["latency"])[int(0.99*len(m["latency"]))]
            print(f"{ver:12s}  n={m['requests']:4d}  err={err_rate:.2%}  p99={p99:.1f}ms")

router = CanaryRouter(canary_pct=0.1)
for i in range(500):
    uid  = str(random.randint(1, 50))
    ver  = router.route(uid)
    lat  = random.gauss(20 if ver=="production" else 25, 5)
    err  = random.random() < (0.01 if ver=="production" else 0.03)
    router.record(ver, lat, err)
router.health_check()
concepts.plain: Canary deployment gradually exposes a new model to a small fraction of real users before switching everyone over. If problems appear, only that small fraction is affected and you can roll back immediately. Safer than pushing to 100% at once — catch real production issues before they affect everyone.

concepts.title: Distributed Training
concepts.short: Training a model across multiple GPUs or machines to handle models or datasets that exceed a single device's capacity.
concepts.body:
Distributed training is necessary when either the model or the dataset does not fit on a single device, or when training time must be reduced by using more hardware in parallel. Data parallelism replicates the full model on each device, splits the batch across devices, performs forward and backward passes independently, then synchronises gradients — each device applies the same gradient update so all replicas stay identical. DistributedDataParallel (DDP) in PyTorch uses NCCL's AllReduce to synchronise gradients efficiently.

Model parallelism splits the model itself across devices. Tensor parallelism splits individual layers (e.g., the attention head computation across GPUs). Pipeline parallelism assigns sequential model stages to different devices and uses micro-batching to keep all stages busy. For models that exceed a single GPU's memory (70B+ parameters), some form of model parallelism is required. ZeRO (Zero Redundancy Optimizer, in DeepSpeed) shards optimizer states, gradients, and optionally parameters across data-parallel replicas, dramatically reducing per-GPU memory without the communication overhead of naive model parallelism.

FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel, PyTorch native) is ZeRO stage 3 — each GPU holds only a fragment of the parameters and gathers them on demand during forward/backward. This enables training models that are much larger than a single GPU's memory using only data parallelism communication patterns (AllGather + ReduceScatter instead of point-to-point). For multi-node training, the communication topology (NVLink intra-node, InfiniBand inter-node) dramatically affects scaling efficiency. A poorly networked cluster may scale at only 50% efficiency with 8 nodes versus nearly 100% on a well-connected cluster.
concepts.code_example:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.distributed as dist
from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP

# DDP training skeleton (launch with: torchrun --nproc_per_node=2 script.py)
